From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Sports played with hockey sticks

This article is about various hockey sports. For other uses, see [Hockey (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hockey_(disambiguation) "Hockey (disambiguation)").

**Hockey** is a family of [stick sports](/wiki/List_of_stick_sports "List of stick sports") where two opposing teams use [hockey sticks](/wiki/Hockey_stick "Hockey stick") to propel a ball or disk into a goal. There are many types of hockey, and the individual sports vary in rules, numbers of players, apparel, and playing surface. Hockey includes both summer and winter variations that may be played on an outdoor field, sheet of ice, or an indoor gymnasium. Some forms of hockey require skates, either [inline](/wiki/Inline_skates "Inline skates"), [roller](/wiki/Roller_skates "Roller skates") or [ice](/wiki/Ice_skates "Ice skates"), while others do not. The various games are usually distinguished by proceeding the word *hockey* with a qualifier, as in *[field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey")*, *[ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey")*, *[roller hockey](/wiki/Roller_hockey "Roller hockey")*, *[rink hockey](/wiki/Rink_hockey "Rink hockey")*, or *[floor hockey](/wiki/Floor_hockey "Floor hockey")*.

In each of these sports, two teams play against each other by trying to manoeuvre the object of play, either a type of ball or a [disk](/wiki/Disk_(mathematics) "Disk (mathematics)") (such as a [puck](/wiki/Hockey_puck "Hockey puck")), into the opponent's goal using a [hockey stick](/wiki/Hockey_stick "Hockey stick"). Two notable exceptions use a straight stick and an open disk (still referred to as a *puck*) with a hole in the center instead. The first case is a style of floor hockey whose rules were codified in 1936 during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_Canada "Great Depression in Canada") by Canada's [Sam Jacks](/wiki/Sam_Jacks "Sam Jacks"). The second case involves a variant which was later modified in roughly the 1970s to make a related game that would be considered suitable for inclusion as a team sport in the newly emerging Special Olympics. The floor game of [gym ringette](/wiki/Ringette#Gym_Ringette "Ringette"), though related to floor hockey, is not a true variant because it was designed in the 1990s and modelled on the Canadian ice skating team sport of [ringette](/wiki/Ringette "Ringette"), which was invented in Canada in 1963. Ringette was also invented by Sam Jacks, the same Canadian who codified the rules for the open disk style of floor hockey in 1936.

Certain sports which share general characteristics with the forms of hockey but are not generally referred to as hockey include [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse"), [hurling](/wiki/Hurling "Hurling"), [camogie](/wiki/Camogie "Camogie"), and [shinty](/wiki/Shinty "Shinty").

## Etymology

The first recorded use of the word *hockey* is in the 1773 book *Juvenile Sports and Pastimes, to Which Are Prefixed, Memoirs of the Author: Including a New Mode of Infant Education* by Richard Johnson (Pseud. Master Michel Angelo), whose chapter XI was titled "New Improvements on the Game of Hockey".[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGidénHoudaMartel201450-1) The belief that hockey was mentioned in a 1363 proclamation by [King Edward III of England](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III")[[2]](#cite_note-2) is based on modern translations of the proclamation, which was originally in Latin and explicitly forbade the games "Pilam Manualem, Pedivam, & Bacularem: & ad Canibucam & Gallorum Pugnam".[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The English historian and biographer [John Strype](/wiki/John_Strype "John Strype") did not use the word "hockey" when he translated the proclamation in 1720, instead translating "Canibucam" as "Cambuck";[[5]](#cite_note-5) this may have referred to either an early form of hockey or a game more similar to golf or croquet.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
The word *hockey* itself is of unknown origin. One supposition is that it is a derivative of *hoquet*, a [Middle French](/wiki/Middle_French "Middle French") word for a [shepherd's stave](/wiki/Shepherd%27s_crook "Shepherd's crook").[[7]](#cite_note-7) The curved, or "hooked" ends of the sticks used for hockey would indeed have resembled these staves, and similar folk etymologies exist for the bat-and-ball sports of [Croquet](/wiki/Croquet "Croquet") and [Cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"). Another supposition derives from the known use of cork bungs (stoppers), in place of wooden balls to play the game. The stoppers came from barrels containing "hock" ale, also called "hocky".[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGidénHoudaMartel2014235-8)

### Modern usage

In most of the world, the term *hockey* when used without clarification refers to [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"), while in Canada, the United States, Russia and most of [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and Northern Europe, the term usually refers to [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey").[[9]](#cite_note-9)
In more recent history, the word "hockey" is used in reference to either the summer Olympic sport of field hockey, which is a stick and ball game, and the winter ice team skating sports of [bandy](/wiki/Bandy "Bandy") and [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"). This is because field hockey and other stick and ball sports and their related variants preceded games which would eventually be played on ice with ice skates, namely bandy and ice hockey, as well as sports involving dry floors such as [roller hockey](/wiki/Roller_hockey "Roller hockey") and [floor hockey](/wiki/Floor_hockey "Floor hockey"). However, the "hockey" referred to in common parlance often depends on locale, geography, and the size and popularity of the sport involved. For example, in Europe, "hockey" more typically refers to field hockey, whereas in Canada, it typically refers to ice hockey. In the case of [bandy](/wiki/Bandy "Bandy"), the game was initially called "hockey on the ice" and preceded the organization and development of ice hockey, but was officially changed to "bandy" in the early 20th century to avoid confusion with ice hockey, a separate sport. Bandy, while related to other hockey games, derives some of its inspiration from [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football").

## History

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Antiek_hockey.JPG/250px-Antiek_hockey.JPG)](/wiki/File:Antiek_hockey.JPG)

*Bas relief* approx. 600 BC, in the [National Archaeological Museum of Athens](/wiki/National_Archaeological_Museum_of_Athens "National Archaeological Museum of Athens")

Games played with [curved sticks](/wiki/Hockey_stick "Hockey stick") and a ball can be found in the histories of many cultures. In [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), 4000-year-old carvings feature teams with sticks and a projectile, [hurling](/wiki/Hurling "Hurling") dates to before 1272 BC in Ireland, and there is a depiction from approximately 600 BC in [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), where the game may have been called *kerētízein* (κερητίζειν) because it was played with a horn or horn-like stick (**kéras**, κέρας).[[10]](#cite_note-10) In [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia "Inner Mongolia"), the [Daur people](/wiki/Daur_people "Daur people") have been playing *[beikou](/wiki/Beikou "Beikou")*, a game similar to modern field hockey, for about 1,000 years.[[11]](#cite_note-nyt-11) The [Tio](/wiki/Tio_people "Tio people") of the Republic of Congo played a hockey-like game with a ball called *kuli*.[[12]](#cite_note-:4-12): 164 Most evidence of hockey-like games during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") is found in legislation concerning sports and games. The [Galway](/wiki/Galway "Galway") Statute enacted in Ireland in 1527 banned certain types of ball games, including games using "hooked" (written "hockie", similar to "hooky") sticks.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

> ...at no tyme to use ne occupye the horlinge of the litill balle with hockie stickes or staves, nor use no hande ball to play withoute walles, but only greate foote balle[[14]](#cite_note-14)

> Bandy, ». a game, like that of Golf, in which the ad- verse parties endeavour to beat a ball (generally a knob or gnarl from the trunk of a tree,) opposite ways...the stick with which the game is played is crook'd at the end.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

By the 19th century, the various forms and divisions of historic games began to differentiate and coalesce into the individual sports defined today. Organizations dedicated to the codification of rules and regulations began to form, and national and international bodies sprang up to manage domestic and international competition.

## Subtypes

### Field hockey

Main article: [Field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey")

Field hockey is played on gravel, natural grass, or sand-based or water-based [artificial turf](/wiki/Artificial_turf "Artificial turf"), with a small, hard ball approximately 73 millimetres (2.9 in) in diameter. The game is popular among both men and women in many parts of the world, particularly in Europe, Asia, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, and [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"). In most countries, the game is played between single-sex sides, although they can be mixed-sex.

The governing body is the 126-member [International Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Hockey_Federation "International Hockey Federation") (FIH). Men's field hockey has been played at every [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the Summer Olympics") since 1908 except for 1912 and 1924, while women's field hockey has been played at the Summer Olympic Games since 1980.
Modern [field hockey sticks](/wiki/Field_hockey_stick "Field hockey stick") are constructed of a composite of wood, glass fibre or carbon fibre (sometimes both) and are J-shaped, with a curved hook at the playing end, a flat surface on the playing side and a curved surface on the rear side. All sticks are right-handed – left-handed sticks are not permitted.
While field hockey in its current form appeared in mid-18th century England, primarily in schools, it was not until the first half of the 19th century that it became firmly established. The first club was created in 1849 at [Blackheath](/wiki/Blackheath,_London "Blackheath, London") in south-east London. Field hockey is the [national sport](/wiki/National_sport "National sport") of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan").[[16]](#cite_note-16) It was the national sport of India until the [Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports](/wiki/Ministry_of_Youth_Affairs_and_Sports_(India) "Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports (India)") declared in August 2012 that India has no national sport.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

#### Indoor hockey

Main article: [Indoor hockey](/wiki/Indoor_hockey "Indoor hockey")

Indoor hockey is an indoor variant of [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"). It is similar to the outdoor game in that two teams compete to move a hard ball into the goal of the opposing side using [hockey sticks](/wiki/Hockey_stick "Hockey stick"). Indoor hockey is played on a smaller area and between smaller teams than field hockey and the sidelines are replaced by solid barriers from which the ball rebounds and remains in play.

### On ice

#### Bandy

Main article: [Bandy](/wiki/Bandy "Bandy")

Bandy is played with a [ball](/wiki/Bandy_ball "Bandy ball") on a [football pitch](/wiki/Association_football_pitch "Association football pitch")-sized ice arena ([bandy rink](/wiki/Bandy_rink "Bandy rink")), typically outdoors, and with many rules similar to [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). It is played professionally in Russia and Sweden. The sport is recognized by the [IOC](/wiki/IOC "IOC"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) including the variety [rink bandy](/wiki/Rink_bandy "Rink bandy"). Its international governing body is the [Federation of International Bandy](/wiki/Federation_of_International_Bandy "Federation of International Bandy").
Bandy has its roots in England in the 19th century, was originally called "hockey on the ice",[[19]](#cite_note-SBF-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) and spread from England to other European countries around 1900 (the first international match took place in early 1891, when [Bury Fen Bandy Club](/wiki/Bury_Fen_Bandy_Club "Bury Fen Bandy Club") visited [Haarlem](/wiki/Haarlem "Haarlem")). A similar Russian sport can also be seen as a predecessor and in Russia, bandy is sometimes called "Russian hockey". [Bandy World Championships](/wiki/Bandy_World_Championship "Bandy World Championship") have been played since 1957 and [Women's Bandy World Championships](/wiki/Women%27s_Bandy_World_Championship "Women's Bandy World Championship") since 2004. There are national club championships in many countries and the top clubs in the world play in the [Bandy World Cup](/wiki/Bandy_World_Cup "Bandy World Cup") every year.

#### Ice hockey

Main article: [Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey")

[Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") is played between two teams of skaters on a large flat area of ice, using a three-inch-diameter (76.2 mm) [vulcanized rubber](/wiki/Sulfur_vulcanization "Sulfur vulcanization") disc called a [puck](/wiki/Hockey_puck "Hockey puck"). This puck is often frozen before high-level games to decrease the amount of bouncing and friction on the ice. The game is played all over North America, Europe and to varying extents in many other countries around the world. It is the most popular sport in Canada, [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"). Ice hockey is the national sport of Latvia[[21]](#cite_note-NRA-21) and the national winter sport of Canada.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Ice hockey is played at a number of levels, by all ages.
The governing body of international play is the 77-member [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") (IIHF). Men's ice hockey has been played at the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games "Ice hockey at the Olympic Games") since 1924, and was in the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics"). Women's ice hockey was added to the [Winter Olympics in 1998](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics"). North America's [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) is the strongest professional ice hockey league, drawing top ice hockey players from around the globe. The NHL rules are slightly different from those used in Olympic ice hockey over many categories. International ice hockey rules were adopted from Canadian rules in the early 1900s.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPodnieksSzemberg2007198-23)
The contemporary sport developed in Canada from European and native influences. These included various stick and ball games similar to field hockey, bandy and other games where two teams push a ball or object back and forth with sticks. These were played outdoors on ice under the name "hockey" in England throughout the 19th century, and even earlier under various other names.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGidénHoudaMartel2014-24) In Canada, there are 24 reports[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGidénHoudaMartel201424,_25,_248-25) of hockey-like games in the 19th century before 1875 (five of them using the name "hockey"). The [first organized and recorded game](/wiki/First_indoor_hockey_game "First indoor hockey game") of ice hockey was played indoors in [Montreal, Quebec](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), Canada, on March 3, 1875, and featured several [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") students.
[Ice hockey sticks](/wiki/Ice_hockey_stick "Ice hockey stick") are long L-shaped sticks made of wood, [graphite](/wiki/Graphite#Uses_of_natural_graphite "Graphite"), or [composites](/wiki/Composite_materials "Composite materials") with a blade at the bottom that can lie flat on the playing surface when the stick is held upright and can legally curve either way, for [left](/wiki/Left-handed "Left-handed")- or [right-handed](/wiki/Right-handed "Right-handed") players.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

#### Para ice hockey

Main article: [Sledge hockey](/wiki/Sledge_hockey "Sledge hockey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/2010ParalympicsCanadaVsSwedenIceSledgeHockey.jpg/250px-2010ParalympicsCanadaVsSwedenIceSledgeHockey.jpg)](/wiki/File:2010ParalympicsCanadaVsSwedenIceSledgeHockey.jpg)

[Sledge hockey](/wiki/Sledge_hockey "Sledge hockey") or "sled hockey" (now called "Para ice hockey") is the only hockey sport on ice created exclusively for participants with physical disabilities.

[Sledge hockey](/wiki/Sledge_hockey "Sledge hockey"), a variant of ice hockey designed for players with physical disabilities, was created in the 1960s and has since been renamed, "Para-ice hockey".[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Ice sledge hockey, or "para ice hockey", is a form of ice hockey designed for players with physical [disabilities](/wiki/Disability "Disability") affecting their lower bodies. Players sit on double-bladed [sledges](/wiki/Sled "Sled") and use two sticks; each stick has a blade at one end and small picks at the other. Players use the sticks to pass, stickhandle and shoot the puck, and to propel their sledges. The rules are very similar to [IIHF](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") ice hockey rules.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
Canada is a recognized international leader in the development of sledge hockey, and much of the equipment for the sport was first developed there, such as sledge [hockey sticks](/wiki/Hockey_stick "Hockey stick") laminated with [fiberglass](/wiki/Fiberglass "Fiberglass"), as well as aluminium shafts with hand-carved insert blades and special aluminium sledges with regulation skate blades.

### Roller hockey

Main article: [Roller hockey](/wiki/Roller_hockey "Roller hockey")

#### Inline hockey

Main article: [Inline hockey](/wiki/Inline_hockey "Inline hockey")

Though [inline hockey](/wiki/Inline_hockey "Inline hockey") is considered a variant of [roller hockey](/wiki/Roller_hockey "Roller hockey") a.k.a. "rink hockey", it was derived from [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") instead and uses a type of [hockey puck](/wiki/Hockey_puck "Hockey puck") or a ball. Both roller games use a type of wheeled skate but inline hockey uses [inline skates](/wiki/Inline_skates "Inline skates") rather than [roller skates](/wiki/Roller_skates "Roller skates") or "quads".
There are puck-based and ball-based variants. Inline hockey puck variant is played by two teams, consisting of four skaters and one goalie, on a dry rink divided into two halves by a center line, with one net at each end of the rink. The game is played in two 20-minute periods.[[29]](#cite_note-:0-29) The sport is recognized as being governed by [World Skate](/wiki/World_Skate "World Skate") which organizes [FIRS Inline Hockey World Championships](/wiki/FIRS_Inline_Hockey_World_Championships "FIRS Inline Hockey World Championships"). The [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") organized [IIHF Inline Hockey World Championships](/wiki/IIHF_Inline_Hockey_World_Championships "IIHF Inline Hockey World Championships") but it has discontinued. On the other hand, ball-based variant is governed by International Inline Skater Hockey Federation.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

#### Inline sledge hockey

Based on ice sledge hockey, [inline sledge hockey](/wiki/Inline_sledge_hockey "Inline sledge hockey") is played to the same rules as inline puck hockey (essentially ice hockey played off-ice using inline skates). There is no classification point system dictating who can play inline sledge hockey, unlike the situation with other team sports such as wheelchair basketball and wheelchair rugby. Inline sledge hockey is being developed to allow everyone, regardless of whether they have a disability or not, to complete up to world championship level based solely on talent and ability.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
The first game of organized inline sledge hockey was played at [Bisley, Surrey](/wiki/Bisley,_Surrey "Bisley, Surrey"), England, on December 19, 2009, between the Hull Stingrays and the Grimsby Redwings. [Matt Lloyd](/wiki/Matt_Lloyd,_Paralympic_Athlete "Matt Lloyd, Paralympic Athlete") is credited with inventing inline sledge hockey, and Great Britain is seen as the international leader in the game's development.

#### Roller hockey (quad)

Main article: [Roller hockey (quad)](/wiki/Roller_hockey_(quad) "Roller hockey (quad)")

Roller hockey, also known as "rink hockey",[[32]](#cite_note-:1-32) "quad hockey", "international-style ball hockey", and "Hoquei em Patins", is an overarching name for a roller sport that uses [quad skates](/wiki/Roller_skates "Roller skates"). It has existed long before the invention of [inline skates](/wiki/Inline_skates "Inline skates"). The sport is played in over sixty countries and has a worldwide following. Roller hockey was a demonstration sport at the [1992 Barcelona Summer Olympics](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics").

### Street hockey

Main article: [Street hockey](/wiki/Street_hockey "Street hockey")

Also known as road hockey, this is a dry-land variant of ice and roller hockey played year-round on a hard surface (usually asphalt). A ball is usually used instead of a puck, and protective equipment is not usually worn.

## Other forms of hockey

Other games derived from hockey or its predecessors include the following:

* [Air hockey](/wiki/Air_hockey "Air hockey") is played indoors with a puck on an air-cushion table.
* Beach hockey, a variation of street hockey, is a common sight on [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California") beaches.
* [Ball hockey](/wiki/Ball_hockey "Ball hockey") is played in a gym using sticks and a ball, often a [tennis ball](/wiki/Tennis_ball "Tennis ball") with the felt removed.
* [Box hockey](/wiki/Box_hockey "Box hockey") is a schoolyard game played by two people. The object of the game is to move a hockey puck from the center of the box out through a hole placed at the end of the box (known as the goal). The players kneel facing one another on either side of the box, and each attempts to move the puck to the hole on their left.
* [Broomball](/wiki/Broomball "Broomball") is played on an ice hockey rink, but with a ball instead of a puck and a "broom" (actually a stick with a small plastic implement on the end) in place of the ice hockey stick. Instead of skates, special shoes are used that have very soft rubbery soles to maximize grip while running around.
* [Deck hockey](/wiki/Deck_hockey "Deck hockey") is traditionally played by the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") on ships' decks, using short wooden L-shaped sticks.
* [Floor hockey](/wiki/Floor_hockey "Floor hockey"): a variety of games with different codes usually played on foot on a flat, smooth floor surface, usually indoors in gymnasiums or similar spaces.
* [Floorball](/wiki/Floorball "Floorball") is a form of hockey played in a gymnasium or in a sports hall. A [whiffle ball](/wiki/Whiffle_ball "Whiffle ball") is used instead of a plastic ball, and the sticks are only one meter long and made from composite materials.
* [Foot hockey](/wiki/Foot_hockey "Foot hockey") or sock hockey is played using a bald tennis ball or rolled-up pair of socks and using only the feet. It is popular in elementary schools[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in the winter.
* [Gena](/wiki/Gena "Gena")[[33]](#cite_note-genna-33) is a field hockey sport played in Ethiopia, with which the Ethiopian Christmas festival shares its name. The equipment consists of a strong stick curved at one end, and a ball of two kinds: either called srur (made out of a rounded piece of hard-wood) or *tsng* (made by weaving a long strip of leather into a rounded shape).
* [Gym ringette](/wiki/Ringette "Ringette") is the off-ice floor variant of the ice skating team sport of [ringette](/wiki/Ringette "Ringette") rather than ice hockey. It is not a direct variant of the style of floor hockey which helped inspire ringette.
* Gym hockey a.k.a. [floor hockey](/wiki/Floor_hockey "Floor hockey") is a form of ice hockey played in a gymnasium. It uses sticks with foam ends and a foam ball or a plastic [puck](/wiki/Hockey_puck "Hockey puck").
* [Hurling](/wiki/Hurling "Hurling") and [Camogie](/wiki/Camogie "Camogie") are Irish games bearing some resemblance to – and notable differences from – hockey.
* [Mini hockey](/wiki/Mini_hockey "Mini hockey") (or knee-hockey), also known as "mini-sticks" is a form of hockey played in the United States and Canada in the basements of houses. Players kneel, or crouch, and use a miniature plastic stick, usually about 15 inches (38 cm) long, to manoeuvre a small ball or a soft, fabric-covered mini puck into miniature goals. In England 'mini hockey' refers to a seven-a-side version of field hockey for younger players, played on an area equivalent to half a normal pitch.
* [Nok Hockey](/wiki/Nok_Hockey "Nok Hockey") is a table-top version of hockey played with no defence and a small block in front of the goal.
* [Pond hockey](/wiki/Pond_hockey "Pond hockey") is a simplified form of ice hockey played on naturally frozen ice.
* [Power hockey](/wiki/Power_hockey "Power hockey") is a form of hockey for persons requiring the use of an electric (power) wheelchair in daily life.
* [Ringette](/wiki/Ringette "Ringette") is primarily a variant of an early 20th century style of [floor hockey](/wiki/Floor_hockey "Floor hockey"), but played on ice hockey skates and designed for female players; it uses a straight stick and an air-filled rubber ring in place of a floor hockey puck [(open disk)](/wiki/Disk_(mathematics) "Disk (mathematics)"). Though played on ice hockey rinks, the rules and strategy differ considerably from those of ice hockey and bear a closer resemblance to basketball. It should not be confused with [gym ringette](/wiki/Ringette "Ringette") which is the floor variant of the ice sport.
* [Rink bandy](/wiki/Rink_bandy "Rink bandy") and [rinkball](/wiki/Rinkball "Rinkball") are team sports of Scandinavian origin. Both were influenced by bandy, but are played on ice hockey rinks and involve fewer players on each team.
* [Rossall hockey](/wiki/Rossall_hockey "Rossall hockey") is a variation played at [Rossall School](/wiki/Rossall_School "Rossall School") on the sea shore in the winter months. Its rules are a mix of field hockey, [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") and the [Eton wall game](/wiki/Eton_wall_game "Eton wall game").
* [Shinny](/wiki/Shinny "Shinny") is an informal version of ice hockey.
* [Shinty](/wiki/Shinty "Shinty") is a Scottish game now played primarily in the [Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands")
* [Spongee](/wiki/Spongee "Spongee") is a cross between ice hockey and broomball and is most popular in [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), Canada. A stick and puck are used as in hockey (the puck is a softer version called a "sponge puck"), and the same soft-soled shoes are worn as in broomball. The rules are basically the same as for ice hockey, but one variation has an extra player on the ice called a "rover".
* [Table hockey](/wiki/Table_hockey_games "Table hockey games") is played indoors on a table.
* [Underwater hockey](/wiki/Underwater_hockey "Underwater hockey") is played with a weighted puck on the bottom of a swimming pool.
* [Underwater ice hockey](/wiki/Underwater_ice_hockey "Underwater ice hockey") is similar to underwater hockey but played with floating puck on the underside of a frozen swimming pool.
* [Unicycle hockey](/wiki/Unicycle_hockey "Unicycle hockey") is played on a hard surface using unicycles as the method of player movement. There is generally no dedicated goalkeeper.
